questions.question:
An equilateral triangle, a square and a circle have equal areas. What is the ratio of the perimeters of the equilateral triangle to square to circle?

A. 3*sqrt(3) : 2 : sqrt(pi)
B. 3^(3/4) : 2 : sqrt(pi)
C. 3^(3/4) : sqrt(pi) : 2
D. sqrt(3) : 2 : sqrt(pi)
questions.to_find: ratio of perimeters of equilateral triangle to square to circle when all three shapes have equal areas

understand.plain: Three shapes — an equilateral triangle, a square, and a circle — all have the same area A. We need to express the side (or radius) of each in terms of A, compute each perimeter, and then simplify the ratio. The key insight is that for a fixed area, a circle has the smallest perimeter (isoperimetric inequality), so the circle's perimeter will be the smallest.

keywords.explain: Among all shapes with the same area, the circle has the smallest perimeter. This tells us P_circle < P_square < P_triangle.
keywords.example: A circle of area pi has perimeter 2*pi, while a square of area pi has perimeter 4*sqrt(pi) > 2*pi.

keywords.explain: For an equilateral triangle with side a, area = (sqrt(3)/4)*a^2. Solving for a gives a = 2*(A/sqrt(3))^(1/2) = 2*A^(1/2)*3^(-1/4).
keywords.example: If A=sqrt(3), then a^2=4 so a=2.

keywords.explain: Once each shape's side/radius is expressed in terms of A, the common factor of sqrt(A) cancels out in the ratio, leaving a pure numeric ratio.

understand.visual_alt: Three shapes with equal areas: equilateral triangle with side a, square with side s, circle with radius r.

given.aim: find the ratio P_triangle : P_square : P_circle when all three areas are equal

terms.meaning: each shape has the same area A
terms.example: if A=1, all three shapes have area 1 square unit
terms.connects: links the three shapes so we can compare their perimeters
sets up the first term of the ratio

terms.meaning: P_s = 4s where s^2 = A => s = sqrt(A)
terms.connects: sets up the second term; the simplest expression

terms.meaning: P_c = 2*pi*r where pi*r^2 = A => r = sqrt(A/pi)
terms.connects: sets up the third term using the circle's area formula

given.plan: Express each side/radius in terms of A. Write out the three perimeters. Divide each by sqrt(A) to cancel the common factor. Simplify to get the pure numeric ratio.

steps.title: Find side a of the equilateral triangle
Find side s of the square
Find radius r of the circle
Form the ratio and cancel sqrt(A)

questions.formula_note: Three standard area formulas are combined; no new formula needed beyond those.

questions.question: In the given figure, angle Q is a right angle. PS : QS = 3 : 1, RT : QT = 5 : 2, and PU : UR = 1 : 1. If the area of triangle QTS is 20 cm^2, then the area of triangle PQR in cm^2 is ______.
questions.to_find: area of triangle PQR given area of triangle QTS = 20 cm^2 and the specified ratios

understand.plain: Triangle PQR has a right angle at Q. Points S on PQ, T on QR, and U on PR are defined by given ratios. We are told the area of small triangle QTS and must find the area of the full triangle PQR. The key insight is that since angle Q = 90 degrees, the areas are related directly by the product of the ratio of the two legs through Q.

keywords.explain: When a point divides a side in ratio m:n, the sub-triangle's area is scaled by m/(m+n) compared to the whole triangle along that side.
keywords.example: If S divides PQ so that QS:QP = 1:4, the triangle with base QS has 1/4 the height contribution of the full triangle from P.

keywords.explain: For a right triangle with legs along the axes, area = (1/2)*leg1*leg2. Sub-triangles formed by dividing each leg keep the same right angle, so area scales as the product of the divided fractions.
keywords.example: If QS = (1/4)QP and QT = (2/7)QR, then area(QST)/area(PQR) = (1/4)*(2/7) = 1/14.

keywords.explain: area(QST)/area(PQR) = (QS/QP)*(QT/QR) since the same right angle at Q is shared.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 300 260' width='300' height='260'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='300' height='260' class='bg' rx='8'/><polygon points='60,220 60,40 240,220' fill='var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6)' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><polygon points='60,220 60,165 97,220' fill='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' opacity='0.4' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='60' y1='40' x2='97' y2='220' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-dasharray='4,3'/><line x1='60' y1='165' x2='240' y2='220' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-dasharray='4,3'/><rect x='60' y='210' width='12' height='12' fill='none' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='45' y='238' class='lbl ink'>Q</text><text x='45' y='38' class='lbl ink'>P</text><text x='244' y='238' class='lbl ink'>R</text><text x='62' y='162' class='sub mut'>S</text><text x='100' y='238' class='sub mut'>T</text><text x='155' y='120' class='sub mut'>U</text><text x='90' y='185' class='sub ok' font-weight='700'>QTS</text><text x='100' y='135' class='sub ink'>area = 20</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Right triangle PQR with right angle at Q; S on PQ, T on QR, U midpoint of PR; shaded triangle QTS has area 20 cm^2.

terms.meaning: S lies on segment PQ with QS = (1/4)*QP and PS = (3/4)*QP
terms.example: if QP = 40, then QS = 10 and PS = 30
terms.connects: gives the fraction of leg QP used by the small triangle QST

terms.meaning: T lies on segment QR with QT = (2/7)*QR and TR = (5/7)*QR
terms.example: if QR = 70, then QT = 20 and TR = 50
terms.connects: gives the fraction of leg QR used by the small triangle QST

terms.meaning: QP is perpendicular to QR, so area(PQR) = (1/2)*QP*QR and area(QST) = (1/2)*QS*QT
terms.example: the 90-degree angle ensures the area formula simplifies to half-product of the two legs
terms.connects: lets us directly multiply the two sub-ratios to get the area ratio

terms.meaning: the small right triangle with legs QS and QT has area 20
terms.connects: the given value used to scale up to the full triangle's area

given.plan: Compute QS/QP and QT/QR from the given ratios. Use the right-angle property to write area(QTS)/area(PQR) = (QS/QP)*(QT/QR). Invert to find area(PQR) = area(QTS) * (QP/QS) * (QR/QT).

steps.title: Find QS/QP from PS:QS = 3:1
steps.note: S is 1/4 of the way from Q to P along side QP.

steps.title: Find QT/QR from RT:QT = 5:2
steps.note: T is 2/7 of the way from Q to R along side QR.

steps.title: Write area ratio using the right-angle property
steps.note: Both triangles share the right angle at Q; the area scales as the product of the two leg-fractions.

steps.title: Solve for area(PQR)
steps.note: PU:UR = 1:1 (U is midpoint of PR) is consistent with the figure but not needed for this area calculation.

questions.formula_note: No special formula beyond basic triangle area; the right-angle property enables a clean ratio multiplication.

questions.question:
Right triangle PQR is to be constructed in the xy-plane so that the right angle is at P and line PR is parallel to the x-axis. The x and y coordinates of P, Q, and R are to be integers that satisfy the inequalities: -4 <= x <= 5 and 6 <= y <= 16. How many different triangles could be constructed with these properties?

A. 110
B. 1,100
C. 9,900
D. 10,000
questions.to_find: number of distinct right triangles PQR with right angle at P, PR horizontal, and all vertices at integer coordinates within the given bounds

understand.plain: We need to count integer-coordinate triangles where the right angle is at P and side PR is horizontal (parallel to x-axis). Since PR is horizontal, P and R share the same y-coordinate. Since the right angle is at P and PR is horizontal, PQ must be vertical (perpendicular to PR), so P and Q share the same x-coordinate. This reduces the problem to independently choosing x-coordinates and y-coordinates.

keywords.term: right angle at P with PR horizontal
keywords.explain: If PR is parallel to x-axis, the direction vector of PR is horizontal. For a right angle at P, PQ must be perpendicular to PR, meaning PQ is vertical. So Q has the same x-coordinate as P.
keywords.example: P=(2,8), R=(5,8) (same y), Q=(2,11) (same x as P, different y).

keywords.explain: Positions of x_P, x_R, y_P, y_Q are all chosen independently except x_Q = x_P and y_R = y_P. The only constraints are x_P != x_R (otherwise R=P) and y_Q != y_P (otherwise Q=P).
keywords.example: x_P has 10 choices, x_R has 9 remaining choices (must differ from x_P).

keywords.explain: Total triangles = (choices for x_P) * (choices for x_R) * (choices for y_P) * (choices for y_Q), where x_R != x_P and y_Q != y_P.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 300 220' width='300' height='220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='300' height='220' class='bg' rx='8'/><line x1='40' y1='20' x2='40' y2='200' stroke='var(--mut,#5F5E5A)' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='20' y1='180' x2='280' y2='180' stroke='var(--mut,#5F5E5A)' stroke-width='1'/><polygon points='100,100 100,160 200,160' fill='var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6)' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='100' y='148' width='12' height='12' fill='none' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='82' y='98' class='sub ink font-weight:700'>Q</text><text x='82' y='165' class='lbl ink'>P</text><text x='196' y='175' class='lbl ink'>R</text><text x='100' y='195' class='sub mut'>x_P</text><text x='190' y='195' class='sub mut'>x_R</text><line x1='100' y1='160' x2='200' y2='160' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='135' y='175' class='sub ok'>PR horizontal</text><line x1='100' y1='100' x2='100' y2='160' stroke='var(--no,#D85A30)' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='60' y='130' class='sub no'>PQ vertical</text><text x='150' y='50' class='sub mut'>x: -4 to 5 (10 values)</text><text x='150' y='70' class='sub mut'>y: 6 to 16 (11 values)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Coordinate plane showing right triangle PQR with right angle at P; PR is horizontal (same y), PQ is vertical (same x); axes labeled with the constraint ranges.

given.aim: count integer-coordinate right triangles PQR with right angle at P and PR parallel to x-axis

terms.meaning: -4 <= x <= 5, giving 5-(-4)+1 = 10 possible integer x-values
terms.connects: used to count choices for x_P and x_R independently

terms.meaning: 6 <= y <= 16, giving 16-6+1 = 11 possible integer y-values
terms.connects: used to count choices for y_P (= y_R) and y_Q independently

terms.term: geometric constraint: right angle at P with PR || x-axis
terms.meaning: P and R share the same y-coordinate; P and Q share the same x-coordinate; x_R != x_P; y_Q != y_P
terms.example: P=(1,8), R=(4,8), Q=(1,12) forms a valid right triangle
terms.connects: reduces the problem to 4 independent integer choices with two inequality constraints

terms.meaning: total count = (# choices for x_P) * (# distinct choices for x_R) * (# choices for y_P) * (# distinct choices for y_Q)
terms.connects: the four independent choices multiply to give the total

given.plan: Establish that x_Q = x_P and y_R = y_P. Count choices for x_P (10), x_R != x_P (9), y_P (11), y_Q != y_P (10). Multiply: 10 * 9 * 11 * 10.

steps.title: Determine the structure of the triangle
steps.formula_raw: right angle at P with PR || x-axis => PQ || y-axis
steps.apply: PR horizontal means P=(x_P, y_P) and R=(x_R, y_P) with x_R != x_P. PQ perpendicular to PR means PQ is vertical, so Q=(x_P, y_Q) with y_Q != y_P.
steps.note: The four free parameters are x_P, x_R, y_P, y_Q.

steps.title: Count x-coordinate choices
steps.apply: x_P: 10 choices. x_R: must differ from x_P, so 10 - 1 = 9 choices.
steps.note: x_P and x_R are chosen from the same set of 10 integers.

steps.title: Count y-coordinate choices
steps.apply: y_P: 11 choices. y_Q: must differ from y_P, so 11 - 1 = 10 choices.
steps.note: y_R = y_P automatically (same horizontal line as P and R).

steps.title: Apply the multiplication principle
steps.note: All four choices are independent once the structural constraint x_Q=x_P, y_R=y_P is applied.

solution.result: 9,900 distinct right triangles can be constructed.

questions.formula_note: Only the multiplication (product) rule for independent choices is needed; geometry reduces the problem to four free integer parameters.

questions.question:
The area of an equilateral triangle is sqrt(3). What is the perimeter of the triangle?

A. 2
B. 4
C. 6
D. 8
questions.to_find: perimeter of an equilateral triangle whose area is sqrt(3)

understand.plain: We use the area formula for an equilateral triangle, A = (sqrt(3)/4)*a^2, to solve for the side length a. Then the perimeter is simply 3a. With A = sqrt(3), we find a = 2, giving perimeter = 6.

keywords.explain: For an equilateral triangle with side a, all three altitudes have length (sqrt(3)/2)*a. Area = (1/2)*base*height = (1/2)*a*(sqrt(3)/2)*a = (sqrt(3)/4)*a^2.
keywords.example: If a=2, area = (sqrt(3)/4)*4 = sqrt(3). This is exactly our case.

keywords.explain: Given area = sqrt(3), set (sqrt(3)/4)*a^2 = sqrt(3). Divide both sides by sqrt(3) to get a^2/4 = 1, so a^2 = 4, a = 2.
Since all three sides are equal, perimeter = 3 * side = 3a.

understand.visual_alt: Equilateral triangle with all sides equal to 2 units and area = sqrt(3) square units.

given.aim: find the perimeter of the equilateral triangle

terms.meaning: the given area of the equilateral triangle
terms.connects: the starting value used in the area formula to find the side

terms.meaning: standard formula for equilateral triangle area in terms of side a
terms.connects: links the known area to the unknown side length

terms.meaning: all three sides of an equilateral triangle are equal
terms.connects: final step from side length to perimeter

given.plan: Set (sqrt(3)/4)*a^2 = sqrt(3). Solve for a. Multiply by 3 to get the perimeter.

steps.title: Apply the area formula and solve for a
steps.note: The sqrt(3) factors cancel cleanly, leaving a^2 = 4.

steps.title: Compute the perimeter
steps.note: Quick check: area with a=2 is (sqrt(3)/4)*4 = sqrt(3). Confirmed.

questions.formula_note: Only the equilateral triangle area formula is needed; the calculation is a direct two-step solve.

questions.question:
For 0 <= x <= 2*pi, sin(2x) and cos(x) are both decreasing functions in the interval _________.

A. (0, pi/2)
B. (pi/4, 3*pi/4)
C. (pi/2, pi)
D. (3*pi/4, 2*pi)
questions.to_find: the interval within [0, 2*pi] where both sin(2x) and cos(x) are simultaneously decreasing

understand.plain: A function is decreasing where its derivative is negative. The derivative of sin(2x) is 2*cos(2x), which is negative when cos(2x) < 0. The derivative of cos(x) is -sin(x), which is negative when sin(x) > 0. We find where both conditions hold at the same time by intersecting the two intervals.

keywords.explain: A differentiable function f(x) is decreasing on an interval where f'(x) < 0.
keywords.example: cos(x) is decreasing on (0, pi) because its derivative -sin(x) is negative there (sin(x) > 0 for 0 < x < pi).

keywords.explain: d/dx[sin(2x)] = 2*cos(2x). This is negative when cos(2x) < 0, i.e., when 2x lies in (pi/2, 3*pi/2), which means x lies in (pi/4, 3*pi/4).
keywords.example: At x = pi/2 (midpoint of interval), 2*cos(pi) = -2 < 0, confirming sin(2x) is decreasing there.

keywords.explain: sin(2x) decreases on (pi/4, 3*pi/4); cos(x) decreases on (0, pi). Their intersection is (pi/4, 3*pi/4).
keywords.example: pi/4 approx 0.785 and 3*pi/4 approx 2.356 both lie inside (0, pi).

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 340 160' width='340' height='160'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='340' height='160' class='bg' rx='8'/><line x1='20' y1='80' x2='320' y2='80' stroke='var(--mut,#5F5E5A)' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='20' y='74' class='sub mut'>0</text><text x='88' y='74' class='sub mut'>pi/4</text><text x='155' y='74' class='sub mut'>pi/2</text><text x='215' y='74' class='sub mut'>3pi/4</text><text x='270' y='74' class='sub mut'>pi</text><text x='310' y='74' class='sub mut'>2pi</text><rect x='90' y='90' width='128' height='18' fill='var(--no,#D85A30)' opacity='0.5' rx='3'/><text x='92' y='104' class='sub no' font-weight='600'>sin(2x) decreasing</text><rect x='22' y='115' width='250' height='18' fill='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' opacity='0.4' rx='3'/><text x='80' y='129' class='sub ok' font-weight='600'>cos(x) decreasing (0 to pi)</text><rect x='90' y='138' width='128' height='16' fill='var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6)' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2' rx='3'/><text x='92' y='151' class='tag ink'>BOTH decreasing: (pi/4, 3pi/4)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Number line from 0 to 2*pi showing sin(2x) decreasing on (pi/4, 3*pi/4), cos(x) decreasing on (0, pi), and their intersection (pi/4, 3*pi/4) highlighted.

given.aim: find the interval where sin(2x) and cos(x) are both decreasing

terms.meaning: d/dx[sin(2x)] = 2*cos(2x) < 0, i.e., cos(2x) < 0
terms.example: cos(2x) < 0 when pi/2 < 2x < 3*pi/2, i.e., pi/4 < x < 3*pi/4
terms.connects: gives the first interval constraint

terms.meaning: d/dx[cos(x)] = -sin(x) < 0, i.e., sin(x) > 0
terms.connects: gives the second interval constraint

terms.meaning: both conditions hold simultaneously in (pi/4, 3*pi/4) intersect (0, pi) = (pi/4, 3*pi/4)
terms.example: (pi/4, 3*pi/4) is fully contained inside (0, pi), so the intersection is (pi/4, 3*pi/4)
terms.connects: final answer: the interval where both functions decrease together

given.plan: Differentiate each function. Find where each derivative is negative. Intersect the two intervals to get the answer.

steps.title: Find where sin(2x) is decreasing
steps.apply: 2*cos(2x) < 0 => cos(2x) < 0. cos is negative in (pi/2, 3*pi/2), so pi/2 < 2x < 3*pi/2 => pi/4 < x < 3*pi/4.

steps.title: Find where cos(x) is decreasing
steps.apply: -sin(x) < 0 => sin(x) > 0. sin is positive on (0, pi), so cos(x) is decreasing on (0, pi).
steps.note: cos(x) is decreasing on (0, pi) within [0, 2*pi].

steps.title: Intersect the two intervals
steps.apply: (pi/4, 3*pi/4) is fully contained within (0, pi), so the intersection is (pi/4, 3*pi/4).
steps.note: Both sin(2x) and cos(x) are simultaneously decreasing exactly on (pi/4, 3*pi/4).

questions.formula_note: Standard derivative rules for sin(2x) and cos(x) are used; no special formula registry entry needed.

questions.question:
The unit's place digit in the value of 2^17 is ______.

A. 2
B. 3
C. 6
D. 9

understand.plain: Powers of 2 cycle through the unit digits 2, 4, 8, 6 with period 4. To find the unit digit of 2^17, we compute 17 mod 4 = 1, meaning 2^17 has the same unit digit as 2^1 = 2. Alternatively, we can compute: 2^17 = 131072, whose last digit is clearly 2.

keywords.term: unit digit cycle of powers of 2
keywords.explain: The unit digits of successive powers of 2 repeat with period 4: 2^1=2, 2^2=4, 2^3=8, 2^4=16(unit 6), 2^5=32(unit 2), ...
keywords.example: Cycle is {2, 4, 8, 6}: exponent mod 4 = 1 gives unit 2, mod 4 = 2 gives unit 4, mod 4 = 3 gives unit 8, mod 4 = 0 gives unit 6.

keywords.explain: To find the unit digit of a^n, find n mod (cycle length). For powers of 2, cycle length = 4.
keywords.example: 17 mod 4 = 1 (since 17 = 4*4 + 1), so unit digit of 2^17 = unit digit of 2^1 = 2.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 320 140' width='320' height='140'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='320' height='140' class='bg' rx='8'/><text x='16' y='30' class='lbl ink'>Power of 2: unit digit cycle</text><rect x='16' y='42' width='52' height='36' class='hi' rx='4'/><text x='22' y='58' class='sub mut'>2^1</text><text x='26' y='74' class='lbl ok'>2</text><rect x='76' y='42' width='52' height='36' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='82' y='58' class='sub mut'>2^2</text><text x='86' y='74' class='lbl ink'>4</text><rect x='136' y='42' width='52' height='36' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='142' y='58' class='sub mut'>2^3</text><text x='146' y='74' class='lbl ink'>8</text><rect x='196' y='42' width='52' height='36' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='202' y='58' class='sub mut'>2^4</text><text x='206' y='74' class='lbl ink'>6</text><rect x='256' y='42' width='52' height='36' class='hi' rx='4'/><text x='262' y='58' class='sub mut'>2^5</text><text x='266' y='74' class='lbl ok'>2</text><text x='16' y='110' class='sub ink'>17 mod 4 = 1  =>  same as 2^1  =>  unit digit =</text><text x='272' y='110' class='lbl ok' font-weight='700'>2</text><text x='16' y='130' class='sub mut'>2^17 = 131072</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Table showing unit digits of 2^1 through 2^5: 2, 4, 8, 6, 2 — the cycle of 4 restarts at 2^5; 17 mod 4 = 1 so answer is 2.

terms.meaning: the last digit of 2^n, which depends only on n mod 4 due to the period-4 cycle {2,4,8,6}
terms.connects: reduces the problem to a simple modular arithmetic check

terms.example: any exponent of the form 4k+1 gives unit digit 2
terms.connects: maps the exponent 17 to position 1 in the cycle {2,4,8,6}

terms.meaning: when exponent mod 4 = 1, the unit digit of 2^n is 2 (same as 2^1)
terms.connects: gives the final answer directly

given.plan: Identify the repeating unit-digit cycle for powers of 2 (period 4: {2,4,8,6}). Compute 17 mod 4 = 1. The unit digit at cycle position 1 is 2.

steps.title: Identify the unit-digit cycle for powers of 2
steps.formula_raw: unit digit of 2^n repeats with period 4: {2, 4, 8, 6}
steps.note: This happens because 2^4 = 16 ends in 6, and 6*2=12 ends in 2, restarting the cycle.

steps.title: Compute 17 mod 4
steps.formula_raw: n mod 4 determines position in the cycle
steps.apply: 17 = 4*4 + 1, so 17 mod 4 = 1. Position 1 in the cycle {2,4,8,6} corresponds to unit digit 2.
steps.note: Verification: 2^17 = 131072, and the last digit is indeed 2.

questions.formula_note: No formula registry entry needed; the unit-digit cycle for powers of 2 is a standard pattern applied directly.

formulas.name: Equilateral Triangle Area
formulas.plain: A = (sqrt(3)/4)*a^2, where a is the side length

formulas.name: Square Area
formulas.plain: A = s^2, where s is the side length

formulas.name: Circle Area
formulas.plain: A = pi*r^2, where r is the radius
